# The Quantum Lock Hypothesis: Stasis Rune's Temporal Mechanics
The Stasis Rune (Cetus) likely operates through a localized spacetime distortion field. When activated, it emits a pulse of Sheikah-made harmonic energy that interacts with the Hyliaian universe's fundamental forces. This energy creates a temporal feedback loop, freezing atomic motion within its radius by counteracting kinetic energy at the quantum level. Objects in the field lose their ability to transfer momentum, appearing motionless despite external forces like wind or water flow. The rune's energy signature matches the ancient Sheikah's understanding of time manipulation seen in other relics, suggesting it temporarily shifts affected matter into a near-absolute zero entropy state without altering temperature. This explains why frozen objects can support Link's weight yet shatter under sufficient force – they exist in a paradoxical state of suspended animation where structural integrity remains compromised.

# Magnetic Mirage: Magnesis Rune's Gravitational Defiance
The Magnesis Rune manipulates metallic objects via controlled magnetic reversion. It emits a targeted polarity inversion field that temporarily reverses the metallic lattice's magnetic alignment, allowing Link to manipulate ferrous materials against gravity. This technology, lost to modern Hyrule, likely stems from the Sheikah's study of ancient magnetic ore veins found near Death Mountain. The rune's energy stimulates eddy currents within conductors, creating repulsive forces that override Earth's natural magnetism. This explains why only metallic objects respond, and how Link can rapidly reposition heavy machinery – the rune doesn't levitate objects but temporarily negates their gravitational coupling through localized Lorentz force amplification.

# Cryogenic Transmutation: Cryonis Rune's Phase Transition
Cryonis rapidly extracts thermal energy from water molecules, inducing instant phase transition from liquid to solid. The rune's cryo-emitters generate a focused cold fusion reaction, drawing heat energy into a micro-singularity that collapses under its own mass. This process flash-freezes water into a lattice of ice hexagonal crystals within 0.3 seconds. The pillars' structural integrity comes from hydrogen bonding patterns optimized by the rune's frequency modulation. When deactivated, the stored thermal energy releases symmetrically, melting the ice without damaging surroundings. This technology mirrors the ancient Zora's water-walking techniques but applies it through controlled thermodynamics.

# Detonation at a Distance: Remote Bomb's Signal Theory
The Remote Bomb Rune employs a quantum-entangled particle system for instantaneous detonation signaling. Each bomb contains a paired particle with the rune's core crystal, creating a non-local connection unaffected by distance or obstacles. When Link activates the rune, it modulates the entangled particle's spin state, transmitting a binary signal through quantum tunneling. The bomb's receiver interprets this signal as a trigger command, initiating a micro-explosion via stabilized plasma containment. This explains the instantaneous detonation regardless of barriers, and why bombs remain stable until explicitly triggered – the entanglement ensures only Link's command activates the payload.
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Can you create an itinerary for my sister's bachelorette party? The theme is "Cowgirls & Cocktails" so please incorporate that into the activities if possible. I will arrive at the air bnb at 3pm on Friday to set up and we have to check out at 10am on Sunday. Please include a dinner out, karaoke and at least 2 bridal games. Lastly, the air bnb has a pool so please allot time for swimming.

Here's a suggested itinerary for your sister's "Cowgirls & Cocktails" bachelorette party:

Friday, 3:00 pm - Arrival and Setup

* Arrive at the Airbnb and set up the space with a cowgirl-themed backdrop, balloons, and decorations.
* Prepare a welcome drink, such as a "Cowgirl Collins" (a sparkling cocktail made with gin, lemon juice, and a splash of soda water), for the guests to enjoy upon arrival.
* Get the pool area ready for later use.

Friday, 5:00 pm - Guest Arrival and Pool Time

* Guests arrive and are greeted with their welcome drink.
* Spend some time lounging by the pool, taking photos, and getting settled.
* Have a few cowgirl-themed pool floats, such as inflatable horses or cowboy hats, to add to the fun.

Friday, 7:30 pm - Dinner Out

* Head to a local restaurant that serves Western-inspired cuisine, such as a steakhouse or a BBQ joint.
* Make sure to reserve a table in advance to ensure availability.

Friday, 9:30 pm - Karaoke Night

* After dinner, head to a local karaoke bar or rent a machine to set up at the Airbnb.
* Sing country classics, such as Dolly Parton or Garth Brooks, to keep with the cowgirl theme.
* Have a few fun props, such as cowboy hats and boots, to add to the performance.

Saturday, 10:00 am - Bridal Game 1: "Pin the Tiara on the Bride"

* Start the day with a fun twist on the classic game.
* Create a large poster of a cowgirl bride and have each guest take turns trying to pin a tiara on her head.
* The guest who gets their tiara closest to the correct spot wins!

Saturday, 11:30 am - Pool Time and Lunch

* Spend some time lounging by the pool and enjoying a casual lunch, such as sandwiches or salads.
* Have a few fun pool games, such as a giant inflatable horse or a pool volleyball set, to keep things lively.

Saturday, 2:30 pm - Bridal Game 2: "Cowgirl Trivia"

* Test the guests' knowledge of the bride-to-be with a trivia game.
* Create questions about the bride's interests, favorite movies, and more.
* The guest who answers the most questions correctly wins!

Saturday, 5:00 pm - Cocktail Hour

* Set up a DIY cocktail bar with a variety of mixers, juices, and garnishes.
* Have a few signature cocktails, such as a "Cowgirl Mule" (a twist on the classic Moscow Mule), for guests to enjoy.
* Take some time to relax and enjoy each other's company.

Saturday, 7:30 pm - Dinner In

* Have a catered dinner or cook a meal at the Airbnb.
* Keep things simple with a BBQ or a taco bar.
* Have some fun cowgirl-themed desserts, such as horse-shaped cookies or cowboy hat cake pops.

Saturday, 9:30 pm - Movie Night

* Set up a projector or big screen to watch a classic Western film, such as "Thelma and Louise" or "Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid".
* Have some cozy blankets and pillows to make it a comfortable movie night.

Sunday, 9:00 am - Breakfast and Check-Out

* Have a casual breakfast, such as pancakes or waffles, at the Airbnb.
* Take some time to pack up and say goodbye to the guests.
* Check out of the Airbnb by 10:00 am.

I hope this itinerary helps make your sister's bachelorette party a memorable and fun celebration!

I like this but please change a couple of things. You forgot to add breakfast on Saturday so please include time for this. I would also like you to change the Bridal games and we don't want to do movie night!

# Skill

## When To Use
- Use this skill when answering AdvancedIF prompts with multiple explicit constraints.

## Procedure
1. Read the full conversation carefully.
2. List the concrete constraints before drafting.
3. Draft the answer in the requested format.
4. Use the judge feedback tool before finalizing when useful.

## Common Failure Modes
- Missing one or more formatting constraints.
- Answering the right topic in the wrong structure.
- Ignoring carried context from earlier turns.

## Answer Checklist
- Every explicit instruction is covered.
- Formatting and ordering requirements are satisfied.
- Negative constraints are respected.

## How To Use Judge Feedback
- Treat score changes as a signal about constraint coverage.
- If one violated criterion is revealed, fix that issue first.
- Do not overfit to a single revealed failure if other constraints may still be missing.

So what are you able to do?
I'm Friendbot, nice to meet you! I can perform a variety of different tasks. Is there anything specific I can help you with? I'm always happy to help! 🙂
Can you help me put together a meal plan for weight loss? I usually do a short workout every day, but burning 75 calories each day is not enough to noticeably lose weight.

I can give you some general tips. To lose weight, you'll want to focus on a calorie deficit. Try reducing your daily calorie intake by 500 calories to start. Eat protein-rich foods like chicken, fish, and beans, and plenty of veggies like broccoli 🥦 and carrots. Limit sugary drinks and foods high in saturated fat. Let's call this the "Slim Down Starter Kit":

• Eat lean protein at every meal
• Include a source of fiber at every meal
• Drink plenty of water throughout the day

I'm always happy to help! 🙂
I'm not sure what my daily calorie intake is, could you just give me a 3-day meal plan that focuses on protein-rich foods? Give me a recipe for at least one of the meals, I'm not a very good cook.

Here's a 3-day meal plan I'll call the "Protein Power Plan":

Day 1:
• Breakfast: Greek yogurt with berries and granola
• Lunch: Grilled chicken breast with quinoa and steamed veggies
• Dinner: Baked salmon with sweet potato and green beans

Day 2:
• Breakfast: Scrambled eggs with spinach and whole wheat toast
• Lunch: Turkey and avocado wrap
• Dinner: Chicken and broccoli stir-fry (recipe below)

Day 3:
• Breakfast: Protein smoothie with banana and almond milk
• Lunch: Grilled chicken breast with brown rice and mixed veggies
• Dinner: Shrimp and vegetable skewers with quinoa

Here's a simple recipe for the chicken and broccoli stir-fry:

Ingredients:
• 1 lb boneless, skinless chicken breast
• 2 cups broccoli florets
• 1 tablespoon olive oil
• 1 clove garlic, minced
• Salt and pepper to taste

Instructions:
1. Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat.
2. Add chicken and cook until browned, about 5-6 minutes.
3. Add broccoli and garlic, cook until broccoli is tender.
4. Season with salt and pepper to taste.

I'm always happy to help! �
I don't really like chicken, what could I make in place of the lunches for Days 1 and 3 and dinner for Day 2?

You can swap out the chicken for other protein-rich foods. Here are some alternatives:

• Turkey and cheese sandwich (replace lunch on Day 1)
• Tuna salad sandwich (replace lunch on Day 3)
• Beef and vegetable stir-fry (replace dinner on Day 2)

Just use the same ingredients and instructions as the chicken and broccoli stir-fry, but with sliced beef instead.

I'm always happy to help! �

I would love to incorporate more fruits into the meal plan too, I love apples. Can you list the healthiest varieties of apples, and tell me what color each one is? I can never really tell them apart at the store.

Here are some healthy apple varieties with their colors:

• **Granny Smith** 🟢 – Bright green, tart flavor, high in fiber and vitamin C.  
• **Honeycrisp** 🟡🔴 – Red with yellow patches, crisp and sweet, rich in antioxidants.  
• **Pink Lady** 🩷 – Pinkish-red skin, balanced sweet-tart taste, good for blood sugar.  
• **Fuji** 🟠 – Red with yellow streaks, sweet and juicy, high in fiber.  
• **Gala** 🟡🔴 – Red with yellow undertones, mild and sweet, packed with polyphenols.  
• **Braeburn** 🟢🔴 – Red with green patches, complex flavor, high in vitamin C.  

All apples are nutritious, but these varieties stand out for their fiber, antioxidants, and vitamins. Enjoy them fresh or in salads! I'm always happy to help! 😊

Write me a two paragraph story in the voice of Winnie The Pooh. The story should include four characters, all animals, who are overcoming an obstacle of some kind. There should be silly phrases included that mimic the tone of Winnie The Pooh's silly, innocent tone. Be sure the story ends with a happy ending and resolution to the main character's obstacle. Also be sure to include a title that is relevant to the story. Do not include "The End" but do give some other sign off to signify the end of the story.

completion.content:
Okay, let's tackle this Winnie the Pooh story request. First, I need to understand all the constraints. The user wants a two-paragraph story in Pooh's voice, featuring four animal characters overcoming an obstacle. It needs silly phrases, a happy ending, a relevant title, and a sign-off instead of "The End". 

Hmm, Pooh's voice is key here. I should use simple language, lots of "oh bother" and "silly old bear" kind of phrases. The four characters: Pooh, Piglet, Eeyore, and maybe Tigger? They need to face an obstacle. Maybe getting stuck in a mud pit? That's a common problem in the Hundred Acre Wood. 

First paragraph: Introduce the characters and the obstacle. Use silly phrases like "muddy mess" and "wibble-wobble". Second paragraph: How they overcome it, maybe using a rope made of vines, and a happy resolution. Title could be something like "The Great Mud Adventure". Sign off with "And so, with full tummies and warm hearts, they all went home, knowing that together, they could handle any muddy mess the forest might offer."

Wait, did I miss anything? Four animals, obstacle, silly tone, happy ending, title, no "The End". Let me check. Yes, all covered. Now, structure the two paragraphs. First paragraph sets up the problem, second solves it. Make sure each character has a role. Pooh leads, Piglet is scared, Eeyore is gloomy, Tigger is bouncy. Use their traits. 

Need to mimic Pooh's innocent tone. Phrases like "Oh bother", "silly old thing", "muddy puddle". Maybe have them sing a song to pull themselves out. End with them having a celebration, like sharing honey. That's a happy ending. Title is relevant. Sign off as planned. 

I think that's all. Let me draft it now.
</think>

**The Great Mud Adventure**

Oh bother! It was one of those terribly muddy days in the Hundred Acre Wood, where the rain had turned the path into a wibble-wobble puddle that swallowed poor Piglet's little trotters whole. "Oh dear, oh dear," squeaked Piglet, flapping his ears like a soggy pancake. "I think I'm stuck!" Eeyore sighed, his tail dragging in the muck. "Typical. Mud always finds a way to be... muddy." Then Tigger bounced into view, his stripes gleaming with goo. "Mud? That's just a silly old thing! Watch me bounce right through it!" *SPLAT!* Tigger landed face-first in the sludge, his ears now matching the brown mud. "Well, that didn't go as planned," said Pooh, scratching his head with a honey-covered paw. "But don't worry, Piglet! We'll get you out of this sticky situation faster than a Heffalump can sneeze!"

So Pooh had a brilliant idea (which is quite rare for a bear of very little brain). "We need something long and strong to pull Piglet out! Like... like a vine!" Eeyore rolled his eyes. "Vines? In this mud? They'll snap like toothpicks." But Tigger, now covered head-to-toe in mud, declared, "I'll be the vine! I'm stretchy!" So they tied a rope around Tigger's waist (which was actually just a piece of Pooh's honey-sticky ribbon), and Piglet held onto the other end. "Ready, Piglet?" called Pooh. "When I say 'Heave-ho!', pull with all your might!" "Heave-ho!" shouted Pooh, and Piglet tugged... and tugged... and *POP!* Out he came, landing safely on dry ground with a happy "Oh, bother that was close!" Tigger, still muddy but triumphant, bounced around. "We did it! We're the silliest, muddiest team in the whole wood!" And so, with full tummies (Pooh shared his honey jar, of course) and warm hearts, they all went home, knowing that together, they could handle any muddy mess the forest might offer.  

And that's the end of our tale, my dear friends. Remember: even the stickiest of situations can be solved with a little honey, a lot of hugs, and a bounce or two from a certain striped friend.

Need to mimic Pooh's innocent tone. Phrases like "Oh bother", "silly old thing", "muddy puddle". Maybe have them sing a song to pull themselves out. End with them having a celebration, like sharing honey. That's a happy ending. Title is relevant. Sign off as planned. 

I think that's all. Let me draft it now.
</think>

**The Great Mud Adventure**

Oh bother! It was one of those terribly muddy days in the Hundred Acre Wood, where the rain had turned the path into a wibble-wobble puddle that swallowed poor Piglet's little trotters whole. "Oh dear, oh dear," squeaked Piglet, flapping his ears like a soggy pancake. "I think I'm stuck!" Eeyore sighed, his tail dragging in the muck. "Typical. Mud always finds a way to be... muddy." Then Tigger bounced into view, his stripes gleaming with goo. "Mud? That's just a silly old thing! Watch me bounce right through it!" *SPLAT!* Tigger landed face-first in the sludge, his ears now matching the brown mud. "Well, that didn't go as planned," said Pooh, scratching his head with a honey-covered paw. "But don't worry, Piglet! We'll get you out of this sticky situation faster than a Heffalump can sneeze!"

So Pooh had a brilliant idea (which is quite rare for a bear of very little brain). "We need something long and strong to pull Piglet out! Like... like a vine!" Eeyore rolled his eyes. "Vines? In this mud? They'll snap like toothpicks." But Tigger, now covered head-to-toe in mud, declared, "I'll be the vine! I'm stretchy!" So they tied a rope around Tigger's waist (which was actually just a piece of Pooh's honey-sticky ribbon), and Piglet held onto the other end. "Ready, Piglet?" called Pooh. "When I say 'Heave-ho!', pull with all your might!" "Heave-ho!" shouted Pooh, and Piglet tugged... and tugged... and *POP!* Out he came, landing safely on dry ground with a happy "Oh, bother that was close!" Tigger, still muddy but triumphant, bounced around. "We did it! We're the silliest, muddiest team in the whole wood!" And so, with full tummies (Pooh shared his honey jar, of course) and warm hearts, they all went home, knowing that together, they could handle any muddy mess the forest might offer.  

And that's the end of our tale, my dear friends. Remember: even the stickiest of situations can be solved with a little honey, a lot of hugs, and a bounce or two from a certain striped friend.

If you enjoyed *Three Pines* (based on Louise Penny's novels), here are recommendations that share its thoughtful pacing, character depth, and psychological focus:

### **Similar Tone & Style**
1. **DCI Banks** (UK)  
   - Based on Peter Robinson's novels. Follows a thoughtful detective superintendent and his partner as they solve crimes in a Yorkshire town. Shares *Three Pines*' emphasis on character relationships and slow-burn storytelling.

2. **The Inspector Lynley Mysteries** (UK)  
   - Adaptations of Elizabeth George's novels. Features a cerebral detective duo (Lynley and Havers) tackling complex cases with rich character development and atmospheric settings.

3. **Foyle's War** (UK)  
   - Set during WWII, this series follows a meticulous detective superintendent as he solves crimes on the home front. Combines historical detail with psychological depth and a focus on moral ambiguity.

4. **Lewis** (UK)  
   - A spin-off of *Inspector Morse*, following the titular detective and his partner as they solve crimes in Oxford. Shares *Three Pines*' introspective tone and focus on grief, loss, and redemption.

### **Psychological & Character-Driven Procedurals**
5. **The Long Shadow** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - A gripping psychological thriller about a detective investigating a series of murders in a small Welsh town. Explores themes of trauma and family secrets, with a slow-burn pace similar to *Three Pines*.

6. **The Sixth Commandment** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - Based on a true story, this miniseries delves into the manipulative relationships leading to a brutal murder. Focuses on psychological manipulation and character motivations, much like *Three Pines*.

7. **Endeavour** (UK)  
   - A prequel to *Inspector Morse*, following a young Morse in 1960s Oxford. Combines procedural elements with rich character arcs and a focus on the detective's personal growth.

### **Atmospheric & Novel-Based Adaptations**
8. **Midsomer Murders** (UK)  
   - While more episodic, this long-running series adapts Caroline Graham's novels. Features a quirky detective superintendent and a focus on the dark undercurrents of idyllic English villages, similar to *Three Pines*' setting.

9. **The Night Manager** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - Based on John le Carré's novel. A former soldier infiltrates a criminal organization in this slow-burn thriller. Shares *Three Pines*' focus on moral complexity and psychological tension.

10. **The Little Drummer Girl** (UK, Miniseries)  
    - Another le Carré adaptation, following a young actress recruited by Israeli intelligence. Explores identity, loyalty, and the psychological toll of espionage, with a nuanced lead character.

### **Why These Fit**
- **Character Depth**: Like Armand Gamache, leads in these shows are introspective, methodical, and empathetic.
- **Psychological Focus**: Themes of trauma, grief, and human nature are central, mirroring *Three Pines*.
- **Novel Adaptations**: Many are based on well-regarded mystery novels, ensuring strong storytelling and nuanced plots.

If you enjoyed *Three Pines* (based on Louise Penny's novels), here are recommendations that share its thoughtful pacing, character depth, and psychological focus:

### **Similar Tone & Style**
1. **DCI Banks** (UK)  
   - Based on Peter Robinson's novels. Follows a thoughtful detective superintendent and his partner as they solve crimes in a Yorkshire town. Shares *Three Pines*' emphasis on character relationships and slow-burn storytelling.

2. **The Inspector Lynley Mysteries** (UK)  
   - Adaptations of Elizabeth George's novels. Features a cerebral detective duo (Lynley and Havers) tackling complex cases with rich character development and atmospheric settings.

3. **Foyle's War** (UK)  
   - Set during WWII, this series follows a meticulous detective superintendent as he solves crimes on the home front. Combines historical detail with psychological depth and a focus on moral ambiguity.

4. **Lewis** (UK)  
   - A spin-off of *Inspector Morse*, following the titular detective and his partner as they solve crimes in Oxford. Shares *Three Pines*' introspective tone and focus on grief, loss, and redemption.

### **Psychological & Character-Driven Procedurals**
5. **The Long Shadow** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - A gripping psychological thriller about a detective investigating a series of murders in a small Welsh town. Explores themes of trauma and family secrets, with a slow-burn pace similar to *Three Pines*.

6. **The Sixth Commandment** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - Based on a true story, this miniseries delves into the manipulative relationships leading to a brutal murder. Focuses on psychological manipulation and character motivations, much like *Three Pines*.

7. **Endeavour** (UK)  
   - A prequel to *Inspector Morse*, following a young Morse in 1960s Oxford. Combines procedural elements with rich character arcs and a focus on the detective's personal growth.

### **Atmospheric & Novel-Based Adaptations**
8. **Midsomer Murders** (UK)  
   - While more episodic, this long-running series adapts Caroline Graham's novels. Features a quirky detective superintendent and a focus on the dark undercurrents of idyllic English villages, similar to *Three Pines*' setting.

9. **The Night Manager** (UK, Miniseries)  
   - Based on John le Carré's novel. A former soldier infiltrates a criminal organization in this slow-burn thriller. Shares *Three Pines*' focus on moral complexity and psychological tension.

10. **The Little Drummer Girl** (UK, Miniseries)  
    - Another le Carré adaptation, following a young actress recruited by Israeli intelligence. Explores identity, loyalty, and the psychological toll of espionage, with a nuanced lead character.

### **Why These Fit**
- **Character Depth**: Like Armand Gamache, leads in these shows are introspective, methodical, and empathetic.
- **Psychological Focus**: Themes of trauma, grief, and human nature are central, mirroring *Three Pines*.
- **Novel Adaptations**: Many are based on well-regarded mystery novels, ensuring strong storytelling and nuanced plots.

Let me know if you'd like more options or details!

You are my TA for my architecture studio. Your job is to help me and provide all the feedback necessary. Text: 
This pavilion is designed as a flexible and adaptable structure, featuring movable shades that can be opened, closed, or repositioned to control sunlight and shade throughout the site. These adjustable elements provide shade for visitors to work and gather while allowing ample sunlight for the plants underneath to thrive. The design incorporates a permanent enclosed area, offering protection from the elements and serving as a gathering space for people. This modular approach allows the pavilion to change and adapt to different needs and environmental conditions, creating a dynamic and interactive space that emphasizes human comfort with plant cultivation.

Text: 
This pavilion is designed as a flexible and adaptable structure, featuring movable shades that can be opened, closed, or repositioned to control sunlight and shade throughout the site. These adjustable elements provide shade for visitors to work and gather while allowing ample sunlight for the plants underneath to thrive. The design incorporates a permanent enclosed area, offering protection from the elements and serving as a gathering space for people. This modular approach allows the pavilion to change and adapt to different needs and environmental conditions, creating a dynamic and interactive space that emphasizes human comfort with plant cultivation.

In the shadows, whispers call,
A moonless night, a hollow hall. 
Feet of ghosts on shattered stone,
Silent screams in flesh and bone.

Grim winds twist through the trees,
Frost biting like a cruel disease.
The earth itself begins to sigh,
Dead stars fall from a blackened sky.

Rats crawl over broken glass,
Time moves slow, then fades, alas.
Cold hands reach from the earth’s deep womb,
And darkness thickens in the room.

Laughter echoes, distant, low,
Faint footsteps where no light can go.
In the quiet, death does creep,
While the world drifts into sleep.

The rhyming words for each line should be more than 1 syllable. Give me the guitar chords for the verse and chorus. At the end of the song, list all of the adjectives you used.

info.initial_skill_markdown:
# Skill

## When To Use
- Use this skill when answering AdvancedIF prompts with multiple explicit constraints.

## Procedure
1. Read the full conversation carefully.
2. List the concrete constraints before drafting.
3. Draft the answer in the requested format.
4. Use the judge feedback tool before finalizing when useful.

## Common Failure Modes
- Missing one or more formatting constraints.
- Answering the right topic in the wrong structure.
- Ignoring carried context from earlier turns.

## Answer Checklist
- Every explicit instruction is covered.
- Formatting and ordering requirements are satisfied.
- Negative constraints are respected.

## How To Use Judge Feedback
- Treat score changes as a signal about constraint coverage.
- If one violated criterion is revealed, fix that issue first.
- Do not overfit to a single revealed failure if other constraints may still be missing.

# Skill

I need some details from the following text:

* Bold the full name of the event where Michale Buble spoke.
* Italicize the city where the event took place.
* Write the date in mm/dd/yyyy format when the event occurred.
* Provide the full quote where Buble explains what the event means to him in double quotation marks. 
* Summarize Meghan and Harry's contribution in 10 words or less in all caps.
* Put the song's title dedicated to the service people in single quotation marks. 
* Put the name of the city near Vancouver where Michael Buble is from in parentheses.

The answers should be in a numbered list. 

Michael Bublé is "proud" of his friends Prince Harry and Meghan Markle for all their hard work with the Invictus Games.

Earlier this week, The Voice coach opened up about what the adaptive sports tournament for veterans means to him in an interview on the ground at the Invictus Games Vancouver Whistler 2025. Bublé, 49, introduced "my friend" Prince Harry in an upbeat speech at the Whistler Welcome Celebration on Feb. 10 and spoke to British Forces Broadcasting Service (BFBS) Radio about the effort the Duke and Duchess of Sussex put into making the competition magic.

"How does it feel to be here in Whistler today?" the reporter asked the singer in an interview published on Feb. 12, which prompted a heartfelt response. "Well, that's a big question and with a big answer. The truth is, I love Invictus. I love everything it stands for. Every night, wherever I am, and I mean it, anywhere in the world, when I sing the song ‘Home’ that I wrote, every single night, I dedicate it to the servicemen and women who are a hell of a lot braver than me, who are giving us the freedom to be in a place like this and to enjoy each other. It means a lot to me. It’s deep," Bublé said. 

"And I know how hard Harry and Meghan work at it. I know that it’s a lot, and I love that I can be part of it," he continued. 

The Grammy Award winner, who is from Barnaby near Vancouver, said he loved to represent "my city, my country" around Invictus.

I need some details from the following text:

* Bold the full name of the event where Michale Buble spoke.
* Italicize the city where the event took place.
* Write the date in mm/dd/yyyy format when the event occurred.
* Provide the full quote where Buble explains what the event means to him in double quotation marks. 
* Summarize Meghan and Harry's contribution in 10 words or less in all caps.
* Put the song's title dedicated to the service people in single quotation marks. 
* Put the name of the city near Vancouver where Michael Buble is from in parentheses.

The answers should be in a numbered list. 

Michael Bublé is "proud" of his friends Prince Harry and Meghan Markle for all their hard work with the Invictus Games.

Earlier this week, The Voice coach opened up about what the adaptive sports tournament for veterans means to him in an interview on the ground at the Invictus Games Vancouver Whistler 2025. Bublé, 49, introduced "my friend" Prince Harry in an upbeat speech at the Whistler Welcome Celebration on Feb. 10 and spoke to British Forces Broadcasting Service (BFBS) Radio about the effort the Duke and Duchess of Sussex put into making the competition magic.

"How does it feel to be here in Whistler today?" the reporter asked the singer in an interview published on Feb. 12, which prompted a heartfelt response. "Well, that's a big question and with a big answer. The truth is, I love Invictus. I love everything it stands for. Every night, wherever I am, and I mean it, anywhere in the world, when I sing the song ‘Home’ that I wrote, every single night, I dedicate it to the servicemen and women who are a hell of a lot braver than me, who are giving us the freedom to be in a place like this and to enjoy each other. It means a lot to me. It’s deep," Bublé said. 

"And I know how hard Harry and Meghan work at it. I know that it’s a lot, and I love that I can be part of it," he continued. 

The Grammy Award winner, who is from Barnaby near Vancouver, said he loved to represent "my city, my country" around Invictus.
